# BLUE LOBSTER — 4 MINUTE PRESENTATION SCRIPT

### RACHEL — [Slide 3: Did You Know] (~10 sec)
"Hi everyone. Quick fun fact before we start — did you know lobsters swim backwards? "

Video plays.

"Believe it or not, they do. But a bigger problem is, Lobsters swim towards Atlantic Canada!"
(Hand to Presenter 1.)

### PRESENTER 1 — [Slides 1 & 2: Title + Team] (~20 sec)
"We're Team Blue Lobster. Our project forecasts the future of Atlantic Canada's lobster habitat as our oceans warm. Quick intros — I'm Ekin, and this is John, Ezekiel, Ali, and Rachel. Let's dive in."

(Hand to Presenter 2.)

### PRESENTER 2 — [Slides 4, 5 & 6: The Science] (~60 sec)
"Here's the problem. The Gulf of Maine is warming faster than 99% of the global ocean. And that matters because lobsters are incredibly sensitive to temperature.
This curve shows their sweet spot — they thrive between 12 and 18°C, but above about 20°C they hit thermal stress. Too hot, and they can't survive.
And it's not just adults — it's their babies. This chart shows larval survival peaks at moderate temperatures and collapses when it's too hot. So as southern waters heat up, fewer lobsters survive there, and the population gradually shifts north — toward Atlantic Canada. Not too hot, not too cold — they need just right."

(Hand to Presenter 3.)

### PRESENTER 3 — [Slides 7 & 8: Impact + Problem] (~55 sec)
"So why does this matter? Lobster is huge here. Canada exported $1.21 billion in lobster in 2025. Nearly 56% of fishing businesses are in Nova Scotia and Newfoundland. And this industry is already under pressure — there's a 25% tariff on seafood exports to China squeezing prices right now.
So you've got an industry that's economically vital and already strained — and on top of that, the lobsters are moving. Here's the gap: nobody can tell these fishers where the habitat is going. That's the problem we set out to solve."

(Hand to Presenter 4.)

### PRESENTER 4 — [Slide 9: Tool Gap] (~35 sec)
"We looked at what already exists. NOAA's DisMAP is powerful, but it's U.S.-centered. GMRI's forecasts are great — but they predict when lobsters will be caught, not where they'll be. And Canada's own DFO stock assessments flag climate change as a factor, but openly say they can't yet predict how it'll shift distribution. So across every tool, the same gap: nobody forecasts where suitable habitat is heading in Atlantic Canada. So we built it."
(Hand to whoever runs the live demo — assign this to your strongest speaker.)


### PRESENTER [DEMO] — [Slide 10: Watch the Demo] (~50 sec)
(Switch to the live site.)
"This is Blue Lobster. Each region is colored by lobster habitat suitability — green is ideal, red is unviable. Now watch what happens as I move through the years toward 2050…
(drag the slider)
…you can see the picture shift. We built this on 40 years of real NOAA satellite temperature data, scored against the lobster's known thermal range, then projected each region's warming trend forward.
And here's the part for the fishers — (toggle sub-zones) — we can drill down to specific coastlines, so it's not just 'go to the Scotian Shelf,' it's which stretch of coast holds the best habitat. That's the foresight this industry doesn't currently have."
(Hand to Presenter for closing.)

### [CLOSING] — [Slides 11 & 12: What's Next + Q&A] (~30 sec)
"With more time, we'd integrate the granular DFO coastal data — which exists, it's just access-gated — and add climate-scenario projections for stronger forecasts. But even today, Blue Lobster turns ocean warming data into something a fishing community can actually act on. Thank you — we're happy to take any questions."
